candidates_contributions.candidates_significant_lifetime_contribu: Dr. Margaret Elaine Hennessey is a marine biogeochemist whose four-decade career fundamentally advanced understanding of how the Northwest Atlantic coastal ocean processes carbon and nutrients under a changing climate. She pioneered high‑resolution, year‑round monitoring of carbon fluxes on the Scotian Shelf, integrating ship‑based surveys, moored sensors, and satellite data long before such observing systems became standard. Her work provided the first robust estimates of coastal carbon sequestration capacity for Atlantic Canada, directly informing federal climate policy and regional coastal management. In addition to her research, she played a pivotal role in building collaborative networks between academic scientists, Mi’kmaw communities, fisheries stakeholders, and government agencies to co‑develop science that is both rigorous and locally relevant.
candidates_contributions.place_where_the_work_above_was_completed: Dalhousie University, Department of Oceanography, Halifax, Nova Scotia, Canada; Bedford Institute of Oceanography, Fisheries and Oceans Canada (DFO), Dartmouth, Nova Scotia, Canada; Coastal field stations along the Nova Scotia shoreline (e.g., Sambro, Lunenburg, and Chedabucto Bay).
candidates_contributions.additional_information_about_the_candida:
Over the span of more than 35 years, Dr. Hennessey led and contributed to a series of multi‑disciplinary projects focused on the carbon and nutrient dynamics of the Scotian Shelf, the Bay of Fundy, and the Gulf of St. Lawrence. Early in her career, she helped adapt and validate automated pCO₂ measurement systems for use in cold, high‑latitude coastal waters, at a time when most carbon flux estimates were based on sparse, seasonal sampling. This technical innovation allowed her team to capture the pronounced shoulder‑season (spring and fall) variability that had previously been missed, revealing that the Nova Scotia coastal ocean is a more dynamic and, at times, stronger carbon sink than had been recognized in global models.

She also co‑developed one of the first coupled physical‑biogeochemical models specifically tuned for the Scotian Shelf region, integrating local bathymetry, riverine input, and biological productivity. This model has been used repeatedly by Fisheries and Oceans Canada to evaluate scenarios involving nutrient loading from coastal development, changes in freshwater discharge, and projected warming and stratification. Her publications—over 120 peer‑reviewed articles and several influential book chapters—are widely cited, particularly her 2007 and 2015 papers that quantified interannual variability in shelf carbon uptake and linked it to large‑scale climate indices like the North Atlantic Oscillation. 

Beyond her scholarly output, Dr. Hennessey has been deeply committed to training and mentorship. She has supervised or co‑supervised more than 30 graduate students and 10 postdoctoral fellows, many of whom now hold positions in Canadian universities, DFO, and environmental consulting firms across Atlantic Canada. Several of her former trainees have gone on to lead their own long‑term observing programs, a clear testament to her influence in building research capacity in the region. She has also been an advocate for diversity and inclusion in the marine sciences, co‑founding the Atlantic Women in Ocean Research (AWOR) network and serving as a mentor for Indigenous and rural students entering STEM fields.

A distinctive feature of her work is the way she has woven community knowledge and local observation into formal scientific inquiry. For example, in Chedabucto Bay and along the Eastern Shore, she partnered with Mi’kmaw fishers and community monitoring groups to document changes in sea ice cover, timing of spring blooms, and shellfish health, and then paired these observations with time‑series data from oceanographic moorings. This approach not only enriched the scientific record but also built long‑term trust and enabled communities to use data in their own planning processes. She has frequently presented her findings at town halls, school visits, and public lectures hosted by the Nova Scotian Institute of Science, helping to make complex concepts like ocean acidification and carbon cycling accessible to non‑specialists.

In recent years, as climate adaptation has become a pressing policy issue, Dr. Hennessey’s expertise has been sought for provincial and federal advisory panels. She served on the scientific advisory committee that guided Nova Scotia’s Coastal Protection Act regulations, ensuring that coastal carbon habitats such as salt marshes and eelgrass beds were recognized for their climate mitigation value. She also contributed to Canada’s national greenhouse gas inventory improvements by providing region‑specific estimates and uncertainty ranges for coastal blue carbon, filling a major gap in the country’s reporting. In all of this work, she has emphasized transparent methods, open data, and the importance of long‑term investment in observing systems—messages that have resonated with both policymakers and the broader scientific community.
candidates_contributions.other_honours_bestowed_on_the_candidate:
• Fellow, Royal Society of Canada (elected 2016)
• Member, Order of Nova Scotia (appointed 2021)
• NSERC Synergy Award for Innovation (Team Award, 2014) for collaborative work on coastal carbon observing systems with Dalhousie University and Fisheries and Oceans Canada
• Canadian Meteorological and Oceanographic Society (CMOS) President’s Prize (2010) for outstanding contributions to marine biogeochemistry in Canadian waters
• Killam Research Fellowship (2012–2014) in recognition of her leadership in understanding coastal carbon cycling under climate change
• Nova Scotia Discovery Centre Science Champion Award (2018) for exceptional public outreach and science communication across the province
• Dalhousie University Faculty of Science Award for Graduate Supervision (2015)
• Invited Lead Author, Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Special Report on the Ocean and Cryosphere in a Changing Climate (chapter on coastal ecosystems and carbon cycling)
• Honorary Doctor of Science (DSc, honoris causa), Saint Mary’s University, Halifax (2023) for her contributions to Atlantic Canadian marine science and mentorship.
